what_it_does: Records a coordination rule for parallel builds: when splitting work to avoid collisions, disjointness must be decided by file ownership, not by conceptual role, and shared/hot files must have a single editor; it also records a concrete case where this rule was violated and fixed, plus a corollary that cross-owner interfaces must be verified end-to-end.
observations: Declares a hard rule: one owner per shared/hot file; the other builder must meet that file through an interface, never by co-editing it.
Gives a concrete retrospective example from the R13 composable-mind cut on 2026-06-15 involving suite.py and cognition.py edits.
Shows that a conceptually 'separate' edit is not disjoint if it still touches a file the other builder edits.
Adds a corollary: file-disjoint splits create an owner boundary where interface drift can creep in, so the consumer seam must match the provider seam (R13 verify bar 7).
Uses all-caps emphasis for the rule and corollary.
References related fleet items and proven practices via board:// links.
standouts: The central invariant: disjointness axis is the file owner, not the conceptual role of the edit.
The worked example: suite.py was being edited by both lead and fork until all hot-file edits were assigned to the lead.
The verification corollary: cross-owner interfaces are drift-sensitive seams that need an explicit end-to-end match check.
conventions: Follows noticeboard frontmatter with id, address, type, state, timestamps, and history.
Uses board:// URIs for internal cross-references.
Uses ALL-CAPS phrasing to mark normative rules.
concerns: Assumes substantial external context (R13, suite.py line numbers, verify bar 7, fleet terminology) that is not explained inside the file.
No machine-resolvable code symbols; the examples are narrative rather than anchored to extractable code.
notes: A fleet memory guide that turns a parallel-build collision into a single-owner-per-hot-file rule and a verification requirement for cross-owner seams.
questions: How is 'hot file' status determined and ownership assigned before the split begins?
What is the exact content and mechanism of R13's verify bar 7?
Is the interface boundary rule enforced by tooling, or is it a manual coordination convention?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose (a guide on file-disjoint splits) matches the content.
apparent_themes: conceptual versus physical separation of work
intention_signals: Encode a hard-won coordination rule into the ledger so future parallel splits avoid file-level collisions and explicitly verify the seams where two owners meet.
novelty: It makes file ownership, rather than conceptual decomposition, the atomic unit of parallel work assignment, and treats the resulting cross-owner interface as a drift-sensitive seam requiring explicit verification.
contribution: Captures a specific coordination invariant (one owner per hot file), a worked example of its application, and a verification corollary for cross-owner interfaces that future splits can reference.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard guide stating that parallel build splits must be disjoint by file owner, not concept, with one owner per shared file and explicit verification of cross-owner interfaces.
intention_for_embedding: To record and propagate a coordination rule that prevents file-level edit collisions during parallel builds and guards drift at owner boundaries.
